reasoning: After reading the 4th character, S is the literal abcd.
After the 5th character, S is abcdb.
Continuing after introducing X=bc and reading the next letters, the surface form is aXdXab.
Record the update of S matching the surface form aXdXab.
After appending c, S becomes aXdXabc.
Sequitur’s first rule replaces repeated bc with X.
The repeated digram aX triggers creation of Y.
Replacing both aX with Y gives the intermediate surface form YdXY.
Record S after the aX→Y replacements.
After the final d is read, S’s surface form is YdXYd.
Record S before creating Z = Yd and collapsing to ZXZ.
BPE first replaces all bc with X, yielding aXdXaXd.
Record S after the first BPE merge.
The most frequent pair in aXdXaXd is aX; BPE defines Y = aX before the final merge to Z = Yd.
Both methods first form X=bc and no later pair repeats enough to force divergent merges; they end with the same grammar.
Both identify the repeated pair ca and end with only that rule; no further repeated digrams occur.
String is two copies of bacbcba; both methods build the same nested rules culminating in S=VV.
Both create X=cc, then Y=Xd and Z=Yb, yielding S=ZZXaX with no further conflicts.
With the extra acca suffix, BPE merges Zb→W before collapsing YY→V, ending with S=WWVY; Sequitur never forms Zb and ends with S=ZZVY.
